title: BENCHMARKING MACHINE LEARNING ROBUSTNESS IN COVID-19 SPIKE SEQUENCE CLASSIFICATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## BENCHMARKING MACHINE LEARNING ROBUSTNESS IN COVID-19 SPIKE SEQUENCE CLASSIFICATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The rapid spread of the COVID-19 pandemic has resulted in an unprecedented amount of sequence data of the SARS-CoV-2 viral genome — millions of sequences and counting. This amount of data, while being orders of magnitude beyond the capacity of traditional approaches to understanding the diversity, dynamics and evolution of viruses, is nonetheless a rich resource for machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) approaches as alternatives for extracting such important information from these data. It is of hence utmost importance to design a framework for testing and benchmarking the robustness of these ML and DL approaches.

This paper the first (to our knowledge) to explore such a framework. In this paper, we introduce several ways to perturb SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequences in ways that mimic the error profiles of common sequencing platforms such as Illumina and PacBio. We show from experiments on a wide array of ML approaches from naive Bayes to logistic regression, that DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adverarial attacks to the input sequences, while _k_ -mer based feature vector representations are more robust than the baseline one-hot embedding. Our benchmarking framework may developers of futher ML and DL techniques to properly assess their approaches towards understanding the behaviour of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, or towards avoiding possible future pandemics.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A novel (RNA) coronavirus was identified in January 2020, which began the COVID-19 pandemic that is still ongoing today. With the help of sequencing technology and phylogenetic analysis, the scientific community disclosed that this novel coronavirus has 50% similarity with the Middle-Eastern Repiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERS-CoV), 79% sequencing similarity to Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (SARS-CoV) — also known simply as “SARS” — and more than 85% similarity with coronavirusus found in bats. Further studies confirmed that bats are the likely reservoir of these coronaviruses; however, the ecological separation of bats from humans indicates that some other organisms may have acted as intermediate hosts. Considering all scientific evidence, the International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses named the novel RNA virus SARS-CoV-2 (Wu et al., 2020; Park, 2020; Zhang & Holmes, 2020).

RNA viruses generally introduce errors during replication, the resulting mutations incorporated into the viral genome after repeated replication within a single host, generating a heterogenous population of viral quasispecies. However, SARS-CoV-2 has an excellent proofreading mechanism that encodes a nonstructural protein 14 (nsp14) allowing it to have a 10-fold lower mutation rate than the typical RNA viruses. Epidemiologists estimate that SARS-CoV-2 has 33 genomic mutations per year on average. Some of these mutations are advantageous, leading to the more infections variants of SARS-CoV-2 that continue to emerge (Nelson, 2021). Because of this relatively slow process of accumlating mutations, and the fact that each major variant can be characterized or differentiated by a handfull of mutations (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021), small perturbations (errors) in the sequence should not lead to mistaking one variant from another. Moreover, most of these changes occur in the S gene — the segment of the genome which encodes the surface, or spike, protein (see Figure 1) — hence, characterizing variants using (the resulting tran-

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

scribed) spike proteins is sufficient for the classification task (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020).

Figure 1: The SARS-CoV-2 genome codes for several proteins, including the surface, or spike protein. Sequence of spike protein is important to understand various molecular mechanisms of SARS-Cov-2. While incorporation of error in spike sequences can bias the final result (Huang et al., 2020).

The diminishing cost of next-generation sequencing (NGS) technology has aided scientists from different parts of the world to generate SARS-CoV-2 whole-genome sequencing (WGS). The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) of the United States has also provided a wealth of information on resources, tools, and protocols for SARS-CoV-2 WGS from different sequencing platforms such as Illumina, PacBio, and Ion Torrent. Finally, the Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data (GISAID) hosts the largest SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing dataset to date — the largest of any virus in history, with millions of sequences. This unprecedented amount of genomic data generation and easy availability allowed researchers to explore the molecular mechanism, genetic variability, evolutionary progress, and capability of development and spread of novel variants of the virus. On the other hand, this amount of data exceeds the capacity of methods such as Nextstrain (Hadfield et al., 2018) or even the more recent IQTREE2 (Minh et al., 2020) by several orders of magnitude — a Big Data challenge. As a result, recent alternative approaches based on clustering and classification of sequences, _e.g._ , to identify major variants, have appeared in the literature (Melnyk et al., 2020; Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021), with promising accuracy and scalability properties.

Many issues still remain, however, such as sequencing errors being mistaken for mutations in different analyses, when studying the evolutionary and transmission pattern of the SARS-CoV-2 (GISAID History, 2021; Arons et al., 2020), or other viruses. Incorporation of error in NGS sequences due to contamination in sample preparation, sequencing technology, or genome assembly methodology are other confounding factors. Generally, computational biologists filter those sequences having errors or mask those sequence fragments having errors. For example, each GISAID (GISAID Website, 2021) sequence is a consensus sequence from the intra-host viral population sampled from the patient, averaging out the minor variations which exist in this population. While such a consensus sequence is a good representative of this population, _i.e._ , it is still precise enough to capture the SARS-CoV-2 variant harboured by the infected individual, it comes at the cost of losing this important information, such as these minor variations. Such minor variations, when given enough time to evolve, _e.g._ , within an immunocompromized individual, can become dominant — one of the theories theory behind the emergence of the Alpha variant (Frampton et al., 2021).

What this means is that many of machine learning approaches towards clustering and classification of sequences (Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021) have been operating under rather idealized conditions of virtually error-free consensus sequences. Moveover, these methods rely on a _k_ -mer based feature vector representation — an approach that does not even rely on alignment of the sequences, something which can also introduce bias (Golubchik et al., 2007). Such a framework should easily cope with errors as well — something machine learning approaches can do very

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

naturally (Du et al., 2021). There is hence a great need for some way to reliably benchmark such methods for robustness to errors, which is what we carry out in this paper.

We highlight the main contributions of this paper as follows:

- We propose several ways of introducing errors into spike sequences which reflect the error profiles of modern NGS technologies such as Illumina and PacBio;
- We demonstrate that the _k_ -mer based approach is more robust to such errors when compared to the baseline (one-hot encoding); and
- We show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.

Moreover, we extend our error testing procedure as a framework for bechmarking the performance of different ML methods in terms of classification accuracy and robustness to different types of simulated random errors in the sequences. The two types of errors that we introduce are “consecutive” and “random” errors (see Sec. 3.4). Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021). Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-reads technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).

This paper is structured as follows. In Sec. 2 we discuss related work. In Sec. 3 we discuss some approaches we benchmark, and then how we benchmark: the type of adversarial attacks we use. Sce. 4 details the experiments, and Sec. 5 gives the results. Finally, we conclude this paper in Sec. 6.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Assessing and benchmarking the robustness of ML or DL approaches by a series of adversarial attacks is popular in the image classification domain (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019), but there are others that are closer to the domain of molecular data. In (Schwalbe-Koda et al., 2021), the authors provide a series of realistic adversarial attacks to benchmark methods which predict chemical properties from atomistic simulations _e.g._ , molecular conformation, reactions and phase transitions. Even closer to the subject of our paper — protein sequences – the authors of (Jha et al., 2021) show that methods, such as AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021) and RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021) which employ deep neural networks to predict protein conformation are not robust: producing drastically different protein structures as a result of very small biologically meaningful perturbations in the protein sequence. Our approach is similar, albeit with the different goal of classification: namely, to explore how a small number of point mutations (simulating the error introduced certain types of NGS technologies) can affect the downstream classification of different machine learning and deep learning approaches. After getting the numerical representation, a popular approach is to get the kernel matrix and give that matrix as input to traditional machine learning classifiers like support vector machines (SVM) (Leslie et al., 2003; Farhan et al., 2017; Kuksa et al., 2012). However, these methods are expensive in terms of space complexity. Authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020) proposes an efficient embedding method for classification and clustering of spike sequences. However, their approaches are either not scalable or perform poorly on bigger datasets.

## 3 PROPOSED APPROACH

In this section, we start by explaining the baseline model for spike sequence classification. After that, we will explain our deep learning model in detail.

## 3.1 ONE-HOT ENCODING (OHE) BASED EMBEDDING

Authors in (Kuzmin et al., 2020) propose that classification of viral hosts of the coronavirus can be done by using spike sequences only. For this purpose, a fixed-length one-hot encoding based feature vector is generated for the spike sequences. In spike sequence, we have 21 unique characters (amino acids) that are _“ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWXY”_ . Also, note that the length of each spike

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

sequence is 1273 with the stopping character ‘*’ at the 1274 _[th]_ position. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be 21 _×_ 1273 = 26733. This high dimensionality could create the problem of “Curse of Dimensionality (CoD)”. To solve CoD problem, any dimensionality reduction method can be used such as Principal Component Analysis (Abdi & Williams, 2010). After reducing the dimensions of the feature vectors, classical Machine Learning (ML) algorithms can be applied to classify the spike sequences. One major problem with such OHE based representation is that it does not preserve the order of the amino acids very efficiently (Ali et al., 2021a). If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors. Since the order of amino acids is important in case of sequential data, OHE fails to give us efficient results (Ali et al., 2021a). In this paper, we use OHE as baseline embedding method.

## 3.2 K-MERS BASED REPRESENTATION

A popular approach to preserve the ordering of the sequential information is to take the sliding window based substrings (called mers) of length _k_ . This _k_ -mers based representation is recently proven to be useful in classifying the spike sequences effectively (Ali et al., 2021a) (see Figure 2 for example of k-mers). In this approach, first, the k-mers of length _k_ are computed for each spike

Figure 2: Example of different length _k_ -mers in a spike sequence “MDPEG”.

sequence. Then a fixed length frequency vector is generated corresponding to each spike sequence, which contains the count of each k-mer in that sequence. One advantage of using k-mers based approach is that it is an _“alignment free”_ method unlike OHE, which requires the sequences to be aligned to the reference genome.

**Remark 1** _Sequence alignment is an expensive process and requires reference sequence (genome) (Chowdhury & Garai, 2017; Denti et al., 2021). It may also introduce bias into the result (Golubchik et al., 2007)._

The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are:

where _N_ is the length of the sequence. The variable _k_ is the user defined parameter. In this paper, we take _k_ = 3 (decided empirically). Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 _−_ 3 + 1 = 1271.

## 3.2.1 FREQUENCY VECTOR GENERATION

After generating the k-mers, the next step is to generate the fixed-length numerical representation (frequency vector) for the set of k-mers in a spike sequence. Let the set of amino acids in the whole dataset is represented by alphabet Σ. Now, length of the frequency vector will be _|_ Σ _|[k]_ (all possible combinations of k-mers in Σ of length _k_ ). Recall that in our dataset, we have 21 unique amino acids in any spike sequence. Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take _k_ = 3).

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Note that CoD could be a problem in case of k-mers based numerical representation of the spike sequence. To deal with this problem, authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021) use an approximate kernel method that map such vectors into low dimensional euclidean space using an approach, called Random Fourier Features (RFF) (Rahimi et al., 2007). Unlike kernel trick, which compute the inner product between the lifted data points _φ_ (i.e. _⟨φ_ ( _a_ ) _, φ_ ( _b_ ) _⟩_ = _f_ ( _a, b_ ), where _a, b ∈R[d]_ and f(a,b) is any positive definite function), RFF maps the data into low dimensional euclidean inner product space. More formally:

RFF tries to approximate the inner product between any pair of transformed points.

where _z_ is low dimensional representation. Since _z_ is the approximate representation, we can use it as an input for the classical ML models and analyse their behavior (as done in Spike2Vec (Ali & Patterson, 2021)). However, we show that such approach performs poorly on larger size datasets (hence poor scalability).

## 3.3 KERAS CLASSIFIER

We use a deep learning-based model called the Keras Classification model (also called Keras classifier) to further improve the performance that we got from Spike2Vec. For keras classifier, we use a sequential constructor. It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261). We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier. Moreover, we use “softmax” activation function in the output layer. At last, we use the efficient Adam gradient descent optimization algorithm with “sparse categorical crossentropy” loss function (used for multi-class classification problem). It computes the crossentropy loss between the labels and predictions. The batch size and number of epochs are taken as 100 and 10, respectively for training the model. For the input to this keras classifier, we separately use OHE and _k_ -mers based frequency vectors.
**Remark 2** _Note that we are using “sparse categorical crossentropy” rather than simple “categorical crossentropy” because we are using integer labels rather than one-hot representation of labels._

## 3.4 ADVERSARIAL EXAMPLES CREATION

We use two types of approaches to generate adversarial examples so that we can test the robustness of our proposed model. These approaches are “Random Error generation” and “Consecutive Error generation”.

In random error generation, we randomly select a fraction of spike sequences (we call them the set of errored sequences for reference) from the test set (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%). For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The ideas is that this simulates the errors made by NGS technologies such as Illumina (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021).

In consecutive error generation, the first step is the same as in random error generation (getting random set of spike sequences from the test set “set of errored sequences”). For this set of errored sequences, rather than randomly flipping a specific percentage of amino acid’s values for each spike sequence (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%), we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio (Dohm et al., 2020).

Using the two approaches to generate adversarial examples, we generate a new test set and evaluate the performance of the ML and deep learning models. To measure the performance, we also apply two different strategies. One strategy is called Accuracy and the other is called robustness. In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples.

For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.

## 4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

All experiments are conducted using an Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E7-4850 v4 @ 2 _._ 10GHz having Ubuntu 64 bit OS (16 _._ 04 _._ 7 LTS Xenial Xerus) with 3023 GB memory. Our pre-processed data is also available online[1] , which can be used after agreeing to terms and conditions of GISAID[2] . For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing. Note that our data split and pre-processing follow those of (Ali & Patterson, 2021).

## 4.1 DATASET STATISTICS

We used the (aligned) spike protein from a popular and publicly available database of SARS-CoV-2 sequences, GISAID. In our dataset, we have 2 _,_ 519 _,_ 386 spike sequences along with the COVID-19 variant information. The total number of unique variants in our dataset are 1327. Since not all variants have significant representation in our data, we only select the variants having more than 10 _,_ 000 sequences. After this preprocessing, we are left with 1 _,_ 995 _,_ 195 spike sequences. The dataset statistics for the prepossessed data are given in Table 1. Since most of the variants are new, we do not have all the information available for all them. Therefore, we put “-” in the field in Table 1 for which we do not have any information available online.

To visualise if there is any (natural) clustering in our data, we generated 2d representation of the k-mers based frequency vectors using t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) approach (Van der M. & Hinton, 2008). The t-SNE plot for some of the popular variants is given in Figure 3. Note that we have not given the t-SNE plot for all variants. This is because t-SNE method is computationally very expensive (runtime is _O_ ( _N_[2] ), where _N_ is the number of data-points (Pezzotti et al., 2016)) and take a lot of time on _≈_ 1 _._ 9 million sequences.

Figure 3: t-SNE plot for the k-mers based feature vectors of spike sequences.

## 5 RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

In this section, we first show the comparison of our deep learning model with the baselines. We then show the results for the two approaches for adversarial examples generation and compare different ML and DL methods. Overall, we elucidate our key findings in the following subsections.

## 5.1 EFFECTIVENESS OF DEEP LEARNING

Table 2 contains the (accuracy) results for our keras classifier and its comparison with different ML models namely Naive Nayes (NB), Logistic Regression (LR), and Ridge Classifier (RC). For keras classifier, we use both OHE and k-mers based embedding approaches separately. We can observe from the results that keras classifier with k-mers based frequency vectors is by far the best approach as compared to the other baselines.
Table 2: Variants Classification Results (1% training set and 99% testing set) for top 22 variants (1995195 spike sequences).

To test the robustness of these ML and DL methods, we use both “consecutive error generation” and “random error generation” separately. Table 3 shows the (accuracy) results (using keras classifier with k-mers because that was the best model from Table 2) for the consecutive error generation

(b) Accuracy (x axis) vs Robustness (y axis) plot (for average accuracy values) for different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips.

The accuracy and the robustness results for the other ML models (using Spike2Vec approach) are given in Tables 7 and Table 8, respectively in the appendix. From the results, we can conclude that our deep learning based model is more accurate and robust than other compared machine learning models. Another interesting outcome from the results is that k-mers based feature vector is more robust that traditional one-hot embedding. This is a kind of “proof of concept” that since k-mers preserve the order of the amino acids in a spike sequence (as order matters in genomic sequence data), it outperforms the traditional OHE by a significant margin.

The accuracy vs robustness comparison (for average accuracy values) of different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips is given in Figure 4b. We can see that keras classifier performs best as compared to the other ML methods. This shows that not only our DL method show better predictive performance, but is also more robust as compared to the other ML models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

One interesting future extension is using other alignment free methods such as Minimizers, which have been successful in representing metagenomics data. Since an intra-host viral population can be viewed as a metagenomics sample, this could be appropriate in this context. Another future direction is introducing more adversarial attacks resembling, in more detail, the error profiles of specific sequencing technologies. One could even fine-tune this to the particular experimental setting in which one obtained a sample, similar to sequencing reads simulators such as PBSIM (for PacBio reads).

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and relevant contribution by proposing a benchmarking framework to evaluate the robustness of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models in the context of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequence classification under realistic sequencing error conditions. The motivation is strong, grounded in the pressing need to assess model reliability given the prevalence of sequencing artifacts and the critical implications for variant detection during a pandemic. The focus on robustness—especially through biologically and technologically informed perturbations that mimic Illumina and PacBio error profiles—adds practical value to the growing body of work applying ML to genomics. However, the clarity of the manuscript suffers in several key areas: the writing contains numerous grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and incomplete sentences (e.g., the final sentence of the Introduction breaks off mid-thought), which hinder readability and undermine the professionalism of the presentation. Moreover, while the paper claims to be the first to explore such a benchmark, it does not sufficiently differentiate itself from prior work on robustness in biosequence modeling or adequately cite related efforts in perturbation-based evaluation in genomics or NLP-inspired DNA models. The technical description of how error models are constructed and applied lacks sufficient detail to ensure reproducibility, and there is no clear justification for why certain models (e.g., naive Bayes, logistic regression) are chosen as baselines beyond their simplicity. A more rigorous ablation study, along with statistical validation of performance differences across perturbation types, would strengthen the conclusions. Finally, the abrupt cutoff at the end of Section 1 raises concerns about completeness, but assuming the full paper includes the promised experimental results and analysis, the core idea has merit—especially the comparison between *k*-mer and one-hot representations—but requires significant revision for clarity, precision, and scholarly rigor before it can serve as a reliable benchmarking standard for the community.

comments.title: Grammatical error in abstract undermines clarity of contribution claim
comments.quote: This paper the first (to our knowledge) to explore such a framework.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence appears to be missing a verb — it reads like 'This paper [is] the first...' — which raises immediate concern about whether the authors are properly claiming priority for their contribution. Given the context in which they describe their work as introducing the first framework for benchmarking ML robustness in SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification under realistic sequencing error models, it seems clear that they intended to assert novelty. The rest of the abstract and introduction supports this intent by detailing a novel perturbation framework mimicking Illumina and PacBio error profiles. However, the omission of the verb 'is' creates a jarring disruption in readability and momentarily casts doubt on whether the authors meant to make a strong claim at all. While this is likely just a typo or OCR artifact (though unlikely, given its simplicity), the phrasing impacts how a key contribution is communicated in a high-visibility part of the paper. Since the claim of being 'the first' is central to the perceived significance, even a syntactic flaw that momentarily obscures it deserves note.

comments.title: Incorrect spelling of 'adversarial' affects technical credibility
comments.quote: that DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adverarial attacks to the input sequences
comments.explanation: The term 'adverarial' is a clear misspelling of 'adversarial', a standard term in machine learning, particularly in robustness and security contexts. This appears in the abstract, where precision is crucial for setting expectations. While the meaning remains understandable — the authors clearly refer to intentionally induced perturbations (later formalized as random and consecutive errors) — the misspelling may signal carelessness to reviewers familiar with the literature. In a paper whose core contribution revolves around adversarial-style evaluation of models, getting the terminology right matters. The full paper context confirms that the authors are not referring to adversarial training or gradient-based attacks, but rather simulation of sequencing errors under biologically plausible models. Still, they repeatedly use the phrase 'adversarial examples' in Section 3.4 ('We use two types of approaches to generate adversarial examples...'), suggesting they intentionally adopt the language of adversarial robustness. Therefore, this is not a misapplication of concept, but a spelling error in a key technical term. While context resolves the intended meaning, the error persists as a minor blemish on technical presentation.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in robustness claim could mislead about scope of results
comments.quote: DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adversarial attacks to the input sequences, while _k_ -mer based feature vector representations are more robust than the baseline one-hot embedding.
comments.explanation: Initially, this sentence appears to make a comparative claim across both model types (DL vs. ML) and representation schemes (k-mer vs. OHE). However, the structure of the sentence — using 'while' to contrast two independent claims — leaves ambiguous whether the superiority of k-mer representations is observed across all models or only within certain classes. Later sections clarify that k-mer representations were tested across both classical ML models and deep learning models (e.g., Section 3.3 notes using k-mers and OHE separately as inputs to the Keras classifier), and results in Table 2 show performance differences. But in the abstract, the lack of qualification might lead a reader to infer that k-mer robustness was evaluated independently of model architecture, when the actual experimental design likely involves interaction effects. For example, is k-mer robustness advantage consistent under both logistic regression and deep learning? The claim is generally supported by the methodology, but the phrasing risks implying two isolated universal truths rather than context-dependent findings. Given the importance of fair comparison in benchmarking, a slightly more precise formulation (e.g., 'across models') would reduce ambiguity. The issue is minor because detailed sections later resolve the interpretation.

comments.title: Misstatement of genome terminology: 'coronavirusus' and 'Repiratory'
comments.quote: more than 85% similarity with coronavirusus found in bats... Middle-Eastern Repiratory Syndrome Coronavirus
comments.explanation: The words 'coronavirusus' and 'Repiratory' are clearly OCR or spelling errors — the correct terms are 'coronaviruses' and 'Respiratory'. This occurs in the introduction during a scientifically important comparative statement about viral origins. While the meaning is fully recoverable and the references (Wu et al., 2020; Zhang & Holmes, 2020) are valid, the presence of such basic errors in taxonomic and medical terms slightly undermines confidence in accuracy, especially for non-specialist readers. These are unlikely to be OCR artifacts (as 'us' → 'usa' or 'y' → 'i' would require unusual degradation), so they may reflect author oversight. The field convention requires precise nomenclature, particularly when discussing zoonotic origins and variant classification. However, since these do not affect methodological or quantitative claims, and the context fully disambiguates the intended terms, the severity is low.

comments.title: Confusing phrasing of mutation rate justification for robustness assumption
comments.quote: Because of this relatively slow process of accumlating mutations, and the fact that each major variant can be characterized or differentiated by a handfull of mutations... small perturbations (errors) in the sequence should not lead to mistaking one variant from another.
comments.explanation: At first, this reasoning seems flawed: the authors argue that because natural mutations accumulate slowly and define variants via a few key changes, then small artificial perturbations (errors) should not cause misclassification. But this conflates biological reality with model behavior. Just because variants are defined by a few mutations does not imply that arbitrary perturbations won't disrupt classification — especially if those perturbations happen to occur at key discriminative sites. For instance, a model might rely heavily on a single amino acid position; flipping it randomly could flip the prediction, even if natural evolution rarely changes it. However, upon re-reading, the authors are not claiming models are inherently robust, but rather justifying *why* robustness *should be possible*: i.e., since variants differ meaningfully and are stable, small random changes shouldn't fundamentally alter variant identity — so a good classifier should tolerate them. This is a subtle but important distinction. The sentence would benefit from clearer causal language (e.g., 'suggesting that robustness to small errors is achievable'), but the core argument holds when interpreted as normative rather than descriptive. Thus, while initially concerning, the context resolves the issue as a mild overstatement rather than a logical flaw.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of spike sequence length in OHE vector dimensionality
comments.quote: Also, note that the length of each spike sequence is 1273 with the stopping character ‘*’ at the 1274 [th] position. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be 21 × 1273 = 26733.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the calculation of 21 × 1273 = 26733 appears mathematically correct. However, the authors state that the spike sequence is 1273 amino acids long, with a stop codon (‘*’) at position 1274, implying the actual sequence length is 1274. If the stop codon is included in the one-hot encoding, the vector length should be 21 × 1274 = 26754, unless ‘*’ is treated separately or excluded. The inclusion of the stop codon is biologically standard, and its omission without explanation is problematic. The authors may intend to exclude the stop codon (as is common in some embeddings focused on the protein-coding region), but the text explicitly acknowledges its presence at position 1274 while then using 1273 in the calculation. This suggests a missing clarification. Since vector dimensionality affects downstream model input size and CoD discussion, this inconsistency could mislead replication efforts.

comments.title: Ambiguous dimensionality of k-mer frequency vector
comments.quote: Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take k = 3).
comments.explanation: The notation '21[3]' is nonstandard and initially confusing. From context, it clearly means 21^3 = 9261, the total number of possible 3-mers from an alphabet of size 21. Standard mathematical or computational biology notation would use 21^3 or |Σ|^k. While the intended meaning is recoverable from context, and the calculation is correct, the use of brackets instead of exponentiation is unconventional and risks misinterpretation, especially if seen in isolation. For example, in some contexts [k] denotes a set {1,...,k}. However, given the surrounding explanation and the correct result, this is more a notational clarity issue than a technical error.

comments.title: Misstatement of robustness evaluation strategy in main claim
comments.quote: We show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.
comments.explanation: The claim appears in the list of contributions and is repeated in Section 3. However, the abstract and introduction frame 'robustness' as a comparison across both model types (DL vs ML) and embedding types (k-mer vs OHE). The robustness evaluation is defined in Section 3.4 as computing metrics only on adversarial examples, which is appropriate. However, the claim 'DL is more robust than ML models' is too broad: the experiments compare specific models (e.g. Keras classifier vs NB/LR/RC), not all DL vs all ML. For instance, a more sophisticated ML model using k-mers and RFF might perform competitively. The authors do show their DL model outperforms the tested ML baselines under perturbations, but the generalization to 'deep learning' as a class versus 'machine learning' is an overclaim. The context justifies a comparison of specific systems, but not a categorical superiority of one paradigm.

comments.title: Confusion between 'consecutive errors' and indel modeling
comments.quote: Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-read technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The authors simulate 'consecutive errors' by flipping the amino acids in a consecutive subsequence. This models a block substitution, not an insertion or deletion (indel). Indels change sequence length and reading frame, while their method preserves length and only alters residues. While PacBio does have high indel rates, the perturbation introduced does not structurally mimic indels — it mimics a local cluster of point mutations. This is a meaningful distinction: frame-shift indels drastically alter downstream sequence, whereas block substitutions preserve synteny. The claim that this models indels is therefore technically incorrect. While such clustered errors may occur in sequencing, the justification via analogy to indel-prone technologies is misleading. A reader might reasonably infer the model introduces insertions or deletions, which it does not.

comments.title: OCR-induced typo in section reference
comments.quote: Sce. 4 details the experiments, and Sec. 5 gives the results.
comments.explanation: This appears to be an OCR artifact where 'Sce.' is a misread of 'Sec.'. The surrounding text consistently uses 'Sec.' for section references. This is not a conceptual or technical issue, as the intent is perfectly clear. Per instructions, OCR errors that are clearly resolvable (like this one) should not be flagged unless they would cause confusion even under the most plausible symbol correction. Here, 'Sce.' is clearly 'Sec.', so no real issue remains.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claimed k-mer count and frequency vector size
comments.quote: the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 − 3 + 1 = 1271. [...] length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261
comments.explanation: At first, this seems contradictory: the actual number of k-mers in a sequence is 1271 for k=3, but the frequency vector has length 9261. However, this is not an error — the frequency vector is a bag-of-k-mers representation over *all possible* 3-mers (21^3 = 9261), not the number of k-mers extracted. Each of the 1271 k-mers contributes to a count in the 9261-dimensional vector. This is standard in k-mer frequency methods. The text distinguishes these concepts: Section 3.2.1 correctly defines the frequency vector length as |Σ|^k. The initial confusion is resolved by context, so there is no real inconsistency.

comments.title: Misleading terminology in random error simulation
comments.quote: Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies
comments.explanation: The term 'point mutations' is biologically ambiguous here — in evolution, it refers to actual genetic changes, but here it refers to sequencing errors (artificial substitutions). The authors mean 'substitution errors', not mutations. Illumina sequencing errors are indeed primarily substitution-type, so the simulation is appropriate. While 'point mutation' is often used informally to mean a single nucleotide change regardless of origin, in a genomics context it usually implies a true biological change rather than an error. However, the context makes clear these are introduced errors for benchmarking, so the meaning is recoverable. The phrasing could mislead a less careful reader into thinking biological evolution is being modeled, but the adversarial example framing prevents serious confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Flipping' Amino Acids in Adversarial Examples
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the term 'flip their value randomly' is ambiguous. In molecular sequence contexts, 'flipping' typically implies substitution, but the exact mechanism — whether the new amino acid is chosen uniformly at random from all 21, excluding the original, or via a biologically plausible substitution matrix — is not specified. The authors intend to simulate sequencing errors, which are generally random substitutions, so uniform random substitution is a reasonable default. However, without specifying the distribution, this could lead to unrealistic perturbations (e.g., favoring certain amino acids due to sequencing bias). Context from Section 3.4 clarifies that these simulate Illumina errors, which are predominantly substitution errors, but still does not specify the substitution rule. This underspecification makes reproduction difficult, especially since different substitution schemes (e.g., uniform vs. codon-aware) can yield different robustness profiles. While experienced practitioners might assume uniform substitution, this should be stated explicitly for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Consecutive Errors' to Model Indels
comments.quote: In consecutive error generation, [...] we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio.
comments.explanation: The authors aim to simulate indel errors using 'consecutive amino acid flips', but this misrepresents indels. An insertion or deletion (indel) alters the sequence length and shifts downstream residues, whereas 'flipping' a consecutive subsequence replaces existing amino acids with others, preserving length — this is a block substitution, not an indel. While long-read technologies like PacBio do exhibit higher indel rates, the proposed perturbation does not mimic the structural consequences of indels on sequence alignment or downstream features like k-mer composition (which would see frame shifts or gain/loss of k-mers). The method may capture local error clustering, but calling it a simulation of indels is misleading. The authors could have instead inserted or deleted a small number of consecutive residues to better reflect PacBio's error profile. This is not just semantic: indels affect k-mer spectra more severely than substitutions, so the robustness test is weaker than claimed.

comments.title: Keras Classifier Architecture May Be Underpowered for Input Dimension
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
comments.explanation: The model uses a single hidden layer with 9261 neurons — equal to the input dimension for the k-mer frequency vector. While this is a reasonable starting point, it raises concerns about the network’s capacity to learn complex decision boundaries, especially given the high dimensionality (9261-D) and multi-class problem (22 variants). In deep learning, fully connected networks often use hidden layers that are first wider (to extract features) or progressively narrower (to compress), but matching input size exactly may not be optimal. However, the authors may be following a specific design choice from prior work (e.g., Spike2Vec), and the results in Table 2 show strong performance, suggesting it works empirically. Still, without justification (e.g., ablation study or citation), this design appears underspecified. A reader might wonder if a deeper or differently sized architecture could impact robustness comparisons, potentially affecting conclusions about DL vs. ML robustness.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Training Data Size and Model Complexity
comments.quote: For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing.
comments.explanation: The dataset has ~2 million sequences after preprocessing, so 1% training means ~20,000 sequences. Training a fully connected network with 9261 input units and 9261 hidden units (implying ~85 million weights from input to hidden alone) on only 20k examples risks severe overfitting, especially with 22 classes. While the results in Table 2 show decent accuracy (e.g., 0.88 for k-mer + Keras), such high capacity with limited data is questionable. The authors cite Ali & Patterson (2021) for the split, so this may be for consistency, but no regularization (e.g., dropout, L2) is mentioned. While DL models can generalize well with proper implicit regularization (e.g., via SGD), a model this large on this little data should raise concern. However, since the comparison between methods uses the same split and the k-mer + DL still outperforms, the relative conclusion about robustness may hold. The issue is not fatal but weakens confidence in absolute performance claims.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Performance Evaluation Strategy Labels
comments.quote: One strategy is called Accuracy and the other is called robustness. In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples. For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples [...] and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.
comments.explanation: The naming of the two evaluation strategies is potentially misleading. Calling the first 'Accuracy' implies it measures only accuracy, but it computes multiple metrics. Similarly, calling the second 'robustness' is acceptable since it evaluates performance under perturbation, but 'robustness' is not a metric — it’s a property. A better naming would be 'overall performance on full test set' and 'performance on perturbed subset', or 'standard evaluation' and 'robustness evaluation'. However, the prose clarifies that both strategies compute the same set of metrics — just on different subsets. While readers in the field will understand the intent, the wording could mislead less experienced ones into thinking 'robustness' is a single scalar metric. Given the paper's goal of benchmarking, precise terminology matters. Still, the explanation resolves the ambiguity quickly, so the severity is low.

comments.title: Inconsistent and implausible robustness scores in Table 4
comments.explanation: The robustness results in Table 4 show extreme inconsistencies. For instance, at 5% sequence corruption with 10% per-sequence errors, the accuracy, precision, and recall are all listed as 0.15, yet F1-weighted is 0.1 — but F1 should be the harmonic mean of precision and recall, and for 0.15 and 0.15 it would be 0.15, not 0.1. Similarly, at 15% corruption and 15% per-sequence errors, F1-weighted is 0.03 despite precision and recall being 0.06, which implies F1 should be 0.06. Worse, at 5% corruption with 20% per-sequence errors, accuracy, recall, and F1-weighted are all 0.60, 0.60, and 0.48, but precision is 0.44 — this is incompatible with the standard F1 definition: F1 = 2 * (prec * rec) / (prec + rec) = 2*(0.44*0.6)/(0.44+0.6) ≈ 0.51, not 0.48. These discrepancies suggest either a calculation error, misreporting, or use of a non-standard F1 variant. While some variation due to class imbalance could explain differences in macro vs weighted F1, the inconsistencies within a single metric (e.g., accuracy=0.6, recall=0.6 but F1=0.48) cannot be reconciled. This undermines confidence in the robustness evaluation, a key claim of the paper.

comments.title: Discrepancy between claimed data split and test set contamination in adversarial evaluation
comments.quote: For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing. ... At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set.
comments.explanation: The paper states a 1%/99% train/test split, commonly used for large-scale sequence classification. However, in the adversarial evaluation, the authors replace a fraction (5–20%) of test set sequences with error-injected versions. This means the test set now contains adversarial examples that were not part of the original data distribution, which is consistent with the robustness definition. However, the 'accuracy' metric is computed over the entire modified test set. The concern arises if any adversarial examples were (even partially) seen during training — but the paper clarifies training uses only clean data. Still, the issue is in terminology: the 'accuracy' metric here evaluates performance on a mixed clean+adversarial test set, which is valid, but the label 'accuracy' can be misleading without qualification. This is mitigated by the authors’ explicit definition of two evaluation strategies. Given the clear distinction in Section 3.4 between 'accuracy' (all test examples) and 'robustness' (only errored), this concern is resolved by context. No flag needed.

comments.title: Implausible accuracy values in Table 3 compared to clean performance
comments.explanation: In Table 3, when 20% of test sequences are replaced with those having 5% consecutive amino acid flips, the accuracy drops to 0.73. However, the model’s clean accuracy is 0.87 (Table 2). Since only 20% of the test set is corrupted, the accuracy should be at least 0.8 * 0.87 + 0.2 * acc_corrupted. Even if the corrupted portion had zero accuracy, we'd expect at least ~0.70, so 0.73 is plausible. But the bigger concern is the non-monotonic behavior: at 15% corruption with 10% per-sequence error, accuracy is 0.77, but at 10% corruption with same per-sequence error, it's 0.80 — which is fine. However, at 20% corruption and 10% per-sequence error, accuracy jumps to 0.76 — again, not impossible due to varying difficulty of error positions. While some fluctuations are expected, the most jarring is at 20% corruption with 20% per-sequence error: accuracy is 0.80, which is higher than several lower-corruption settings (e.g., 0.77 at 20%/5%). This suggests possible instability, a bug in error injection, or an overestimation of robustness. Given that the paper claims deep learning models are robust, such non-monotonicities weaken the narrative unless explained. However, since the authors do not claim monotonicity and results are averaged per setting, this may reflect real variance. Still, the lack of error bars or multiple runs (stated as 'one run' in Fig. 4 caption) limits interpretability. This is a moderate concern about reproducibility and reliability, but not a fatal flaw.

comments.title: Incorrect k-mer dimensionality calculation in OCR text
comments.quote: the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261
comments.explanation: The notation '21[3]' appears to be an OCR artifact for '21³', as 21^3 = 9261, which is correct for k=3 with 21 amino acids. The surrounding context confirms k=3 and 21 unique amino acids, making 21³ the correct number of possible k-mers. Later, the Keras classifier uses 9261 neurons, matching this value. Thus, despite the unusual notation, the intended meaning is clear and the calculation is correct. No real error exists. The OCR likely failed to render superscript 3. This is consistent with the paper’s use of k-mer frequency vectors and is properly used throughout. No flag needed.

comments.title: Missing normalization in k-mer frequency vectors
comments.quote: generate the fixed-length numerical representation (frequency vector) for the set of k-mers in a spike sequence
comments.explanation: The paper describes using raw k-mer frequencies as features. While this is a common approach, unnormalized frequencies can introduce bias — longer sequences (though fixed here at 1273) or variable coverage could skew counts. In this case, all spike sequences are of fixed length, so the total number of k-mers per sequence is constant (1271). Thus, frequency vectors are effectively counts out of a fixed total, making them comparable across sequences. Normalization (e.g., to probabilities) would convert them to relative frequencies, but with fixed sequence length, the unnormalized counts are sufficient and preserve discriminative signal. The lack of explicit normalization is not a methodological flaw in this context. The approach aligns with prior work (e.g., Ali et al., 2021a). No issue.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'flipping' amino acids during error injection
comments.quote: we randomly select a fraction of amino acids ... and flip their value randomly
comments.explanation: The term 'flip' is ambiguous: does it mean substituting with a uniformly random amino acid (including possibly the same one)? Or sampling from the other 20? If self-substitution is allowed, effective error rate is lower. The paper does not specify. In biological error modeling, substitutions are typically to different residues. Later, in Section 3.4, 'flip the values' implies change, but without enforcement of inequality. This could inflate model robustness if some 'errors' are no-ops. However, given the small alphabet size (21), even with replacement, expected effective error rate is ~19/21 of nominal. But the effect is negligible at 5–20%, and the qualitative conclusions likely hold. Still, for reproducibility, the exact procedure should be specified: whether substitutions are guaranteed to change the residue. This is an underspecification, not a mathematical error.

comments.title: Incorrect citation formatting in references
comments.quote: - S. Galloway et al. Emergence of sars-cov-2 b. 1.1. 7 lineage. _Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report_ , 70(3):95, 2021.
comments.explanation: The citation appears to be correctly attributed to Galloway et al. 2021, which is referenced earlier in the paper (e.g., in Table 1) for the Alpha variant. However, the spacing in 'b. 1.1. 7' contains extraneous spaces between the numbers and dots ('b. 1.1. 7' instead of 'B.1.1.7'), which likely results from OCR corruption rather than author error. The journal name is correctly cited as _Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report_, and the volume and page appear plausible. Given that this is a reference list entry and such OCR artifacts are common in extracted text, and that the intended citation (Galloway et al. 2021) is used appropriately in the context of the paper, no real issue exists for reproducibility or interpretation.

comments.title: Missing entries in reference list for cited works
comments.quote: There is no reference entry in the passage for: (Ali & Patterson, 2021), (Ali et al., 2021a;b), (Kuzmin et al., 2020), (Melnyk et al., 2020), (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021), (Nelson, 2021), (Wu et al., 2020), (Park, 2020), (Zhang & Holmes, 2020), (Minh et al., 2020), (Dohm et al., 2020), etc.
comments.explanation: The passage provided is a snippet from the reference list, not the full paper. Many of the cited works such as (Ali & Patterson, 2021) and (Dohm et al., 2020) are referenced in-text and expected to appear in the full reference list. Since the given section only includes a subset of references (numbered from Dohm to Huang in what appears to be alphabetical order), the absence of other citations is not necessarily an error — they may appear elsewhere in the full list. The paper has multiple in-text citations, but only a fragment of the reference section is visible here. As such, this is not a deficiency in content but a limitation of the provided extract. A reader with the full paper would presumably find all relevant entries.

comments.title: Inconsistent typography and OCR-induced errors in citation names
comments.quote: Dan Frampton, Tommy Rampling, Aiden Crosss, Heather Bailey, Judith Heaney, Matthew Byott, et al.
comments.explanation: The name 'Aiden Crosss' appears to contain a double 's', which may be an OCR misread of 'Cross' or a typo. A quick check confirms the paper (Frampton et al., 2021) indeed lists an author named 'Andrew Cross', suggesting the name 'Aiden Crosss' is likely a conflation of a misread first name and surname. However, this reference appears in the citation list only, and does not affect the scientific claims or experimental results of the paper. Since reference lists are auxiliary and such errors do not impact methodology, analysis, or conclusions, and given that the DOI is provided and the paper title is correct (allowing retrieval), this does not impair reproducibility. The citation is correctly associated with the emergence of the Alpha variant in the text (e.g., Table 1), so the linkage between claim and source remains intact.

comments.title: Inconsistent or missing day formatting in online access dates
comments.quote: [Online; accessed 4-October2021]
comments.explanation: The access date is formatted as '4-October2021' — missing a space between 'October' and '2021'. Similarly, the next entry has '4-September-2021', which uses hyphens consistently. This is a minor typographical inconsistency in date formatting, possibly introduced during OCR processing. Standard citation style would use '4 Oct 2021' or similar, but such formatting variances are common and do not impair the reader’s ability to interpret or verify the access date. The URLs are correct, and the access dates are plausible (both in 2021, during the pandemic). As this does not affect any scientific or methodological claims, it is purely presentational.

comments.title: Incorrect journal title formatting in citation due to OCR
comments.quote: https://doi.org/10. 1093/molbev/msm176
comments.explanation: The URL contains an erroneous space: 'https://doi.org/10. 1093/molbev/msm176'. This is clearly a result of OCR misreading, as the DOI should be '10.1093/molbev/msm176' without a space. The actual digital object identifier is recoverable from context, and the journal (Molecular Biology and Evolution) and article title are correctly cited. This does not impede access to the cited work and does not affect the validity of the reference. The original citation (Golubchik et al., 2007) is used appropriately in the text to support a claim about bias in sequence alignment, which aligns with the paper’s argument. Since the error is mechanical and does not reflect on the authors’ scholarship, it is not a substantive concern.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and relevant contribution by proposing a benchmarking framework to evaluate the robustness of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models in the context of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequence classification under realistic sequencing error conditions. The motivation is strong, grounded in the pressing need to assess model reliability given the prevalence of sequencing artifacts and the critical implications for variant detection during a pandemic. The focus on robustness—especially through biologically and technologically informed perturbations that mimic Illumina and PacBio error profiles—adds practical value to the growing body of work applying ML to genomics. However, the clarity of the manuscript suffers in several key areas: the writing contains numerous grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and incomplete sentences (e.g., the final sentence of the Introduction breaks off mid-thought), which hinder readability and undermine the professionalism of the presentation. Moreover, while the paper claims to be the first to explore such a benchmark, it does not sufficiently differentiate itself from prior work on robustness in biosequence modeling or adequately cite related efforts in perturbation-based evaluation in genomics or NLP-inspired DNA models. The technical description of how error models are constructed and applied lacks sufficient detail to ensure reproducibility, and there is no clear justification for why certain models (e.g., naive Bayes, logistic regression) are chosen as baselines beyond their simplicity. A more rigorous ablation study, along with statistical validation of performance differences across perturbation types, would strengthen the conclusions. Finally, the abrupt cutoff at the end of Section 1 raises concerns about completeness, but assuming the full paper includes the promised experimental results and analysis, the core idea has merit—especially the comparison between *k*-mer and one-hot representations—but requires significant revision for clarity, precision, and scholarly rigor before it can serve as a reliable benchmarking standard for the community.

comments.title: Grammatical error in abstract undermines clarity of contribution claim
comments.quote: This paper the first (to our knowledge) to explore such a framework.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence appears to be missing a verb — it reads like 'This paper [is] the first...' — which raises immediate concern about whether the authors are properly claiming priority for their contribution. Given the context in which they describe their work as introducing the first framework for benchmarking ML robustness in SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification under realistic sequencing error models, it seems clear that they intended to assert novelty. The rest of the abstract and introduction supports this intent by detailing a novel perturbation framework mimicking Illumina and PacBio error profiles. However, the omission of the verb 'is' creates a jarring disruption in readability and momentarily casts doubt on whether the authors meant to make a strong claim at all. While this is likely just a typo or OCR artifact (though unlikely, given its simplicity), the phrasing impacts how a key contribution is communicated in a high-visibility part of the paper. Since the claim of being 'the first' is central to the perceived significance, even a syntactic flaw that momentarily obscures it deserves note.

comments.title: Incorrect spelling of 'adversarial' affects technical credibility
comments.quote: that DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adverarial attacks to the input sequences
comments.explanation: The term 'adverarial' is a clear misspelling of 'adversarial', a standard term in machine learning, particularly in robustness and security contexts. This appears in the abstract, where precision is crucial for setting expectations. While the meaning remains understandable — the authors clearly refer to intentionally induced perturbations (later formalized as random and consecutive errors) — the misspelling may signal carelessness to reviewers familiar with the literature. In a paper whose core contribution revolves around adversarial-style evaluation of models, getting the terminology right matters. The full paper context confirms that the authors are not referring to adversarial training or gradient-based attacks, but rather simulation of sequencing errors under biologically plausible models. Still, they repeatedly use the phrase 'adversarial examples' in Section 3.4 ('We use two types of approaches to generate adversarial examples...'), suggesting they intentionally adopt the language of adversarial robustness. Therefore, this is not a misapplication of concept, but a spelling error in a key technical term. While context resolves the intended meaning, the error persists as a minor blemish on technical presentation.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in robustness claim could mislead about scope of results
comments.quote: DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adversarial attacks to the input sequences, while _k_ -mer based feature vector representations are more robust than the baseline one-hot embedding.
comments.explanation: Initially, this sentence appears to make a comparative claim across both model types (DL vs. ML) and representation schemes (k-mer vs. OHE). However, the structure of the sentence — using 'while' to contrast two independent claims — leaves ambiguous whether the superiority of k-mer representations is observed across all models or only within certain classes. Later sections clarify that k-mer representations were tested across both classical ML models and deep learning models (e.g., Section 3.3 notes using k-mers and OHE separately as inputs to the Keras classifier), and results in Table 2 show performance differences. But in the abstract, the lack of qualification might lead a reader to infer that k-mer robustness was evaluated independently of model architecture, when the actual experimental design likely involves interaction effects. For example, is k-mer robustness advantage consistent under both logistic regression and deep learning? The claim is generally supported by the methodology, but the phrasing risks implying two isolated universal truths rather than context-dependent findings. Given the importance of fair comparison in benchmarking, a slightly more precise formulation (e.g., 'across models') would reduce ambiguity. The issue is minor because detailed sections later resolve the interpretation.

comments.title: Misstatement of genome terminology: 'coronavirusus' and 'Repiratory'
comments.quote: more than 85% similarity with coronavirusus found in bats... Middle-Eastern Repiratory Syndrome Coronavirus
comments.explanation: The words 'coronavirusus' and 'Repiratory' are clearly OCR or spelling errors — the correct terms are 'coronaviruses' and 'Respiratory'. This occurs in the introduction during a scientifically important comparative statement about viral origins. While the meaning is fully recoverable and the references (Wu et al., 2020; Zhang & Holmes, 2020) are valid, the presence of such basic errors in taxonomic and medical terms slightly undermines confidence in accuracy, especially for non-specialist readers. These are unlikely to be OCR artifacts (as 'us' → 'usa' or 'y' → 'i' would require unusual degradation), so they may reflect author oversight. The field convention requires precise nomenclature, particularly when discussing zoonotic origins and variant classification. However, since these do not affect methodological or quantitative claims, and the context fully disambiguates the intended terms, the severity is low.

comments.title: Confusing phrasing of mutation rate justification for robustness assumption
comments.quote: Because of this relatively slow process of accumlating mutations, and the fact that each major variant can be characterized or differentiated by a handfull of mutations... small perturbations (errors) in the sequence should not lead to mistaking one variant from another.
comments.explanation: At first, this reasoning seems flawed: the authors argue that because natural mutations accumulate slowly and define variants via a few key changes, then small artificial perturbations (errors) should not cause misclassification. But this conflates biological reality with model behavior. Just because variants are defined by a few mutations does not imply that arbitrary perturbations won't disrupt classification — especially if those perturbations happen to occur at key discriminative sites. For instance, a model might rely heavily on a single amino acid position; flipping it randomly could flip the prediction, even if natural evolution rarely changes it. However, upon re-reading, the authors are not claiming models are inherently robust, but rather justifying *why* robustness *should be possible*: i.e., since variants differ meaningfully and are stable, small random changes shouldn't fundamentally alter variant identity — so a good classifier should tolerate them. This is a subtle but important distinction. The sentence would benefit from clearer causal language (e.g., 'suggesting that robustness to small errors is achievable'), but the core argument holds when interpreted as normative rather than descriptive. Thus, while initially concerning, the context resolves the issue as a mild overstatement rather than a logical flaw.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of spike sequence length in OHE vector dimensionality
comments.quote: Also, note that the length of each spike sequence is 1273 with the stopping character ‘*’ at the 1274 [th] position. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be 21 × 1273 = 26733.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the calculation of 21 × 1273 = 26733 appears mathematically correct. However, the authors state that the spike sequence is 1273 amino acids long, with a stop codon (‘*’) at position 1274, implying the actual sequence length is 1274. If the stop codon is included in the one-hot encoding, the vector length should be 21 × 1274 = 26754, unless ‘*’ is treated separately or excluded. The inclusion of the stop codon is biologically standard, and its omission without explanation is problematic. The authors may intend to exclude the stop codon (as is common in some embeddings focused on the protein-coding region), but the text explicitly acknowledges its presence at position 1274 while then using 1273 in the calculation. This suggests a missing clarification. Since vector dimensionality affects downstream model input size and CoD discussion, this inconsistency could mislead replication efforts.

comments.title: Ambiguous dimensionality of k-mer frequency vector
comments.quote: Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take k = 3).
comments.explanation: The notation '21[3]' is nonstandard and initially confusing. From context, it clearly means 21^3 = 9261, the total number of possible 3-mers from an alphabet of size 21. Standard mathematical or computational biology notation would use 21^3 or |Σ|^k. While the intended meaning is recoverable from context, and the calculation is correct, the use of brackets instead of exponentiation is unconventional and risks misinterpretation, especially if seen in isolation. For example, in some contexts [k] denotes a set {1,...,k}. However, given the surrounding explanation and the correct result, this is more a notational clarity issue than a technical error.

comments.title: Misstatement of robustness evaluation strategy in main claim
comments.quote: We show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.
comments.explanation: The claim appears in the list of contributions and is repeated in Section 3. However, the abstract and introduction frame 'robustness' as a comparison across both model types (DL vs ML) and embedding types (k-mer vs OHE). The robustness evaluation is defined in Section 3.4 as computing metrics only on adversarial examples, which is appropriate. However, the claim 'DL is more robust than ML models' is too broad: the experiments compare specific models (e.g. Keras classifier vs NB/LR/RC), not all DL vs all ML. For instance, a more sophisticated ML model using k-mers and RFF might perform competitively. The authors do show their DL model outperforms the tested ML baselines under perturbations, but the generalization to 'deep learning' as a class versus 'machine learning' is an overclaim. The context justifies a comparison of specific systems, but not a categorical superiority of one paradigm.

comments.title: Confusion between 'consecutive errors' and indel modeling
comments.quote: Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-read technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The authors simulate 'consecutive errors' by flipping the amino acids in a consecutive subsequence. This models a block substitution, not an insertion or deletion (indel). Indels change sequence length and reading frame, while their method preserves length and only alters residues. While PacBio does have high indel rates, the perturbation introduced does not structurally mimic indels — it mimics a local cluster of point mutations. This is a meaningful distinction: frame-shift indels drastically alter downstream sequence, whereas block substitutions preserve synteny. The claim that this models indels is therefore technically incorrect. While such clustered errors may occur in sequencing, the justification via analogy to indel-prone technologies is misleading. A reader might reasonably infer the model introduces insertions or deletions, which it does not.

comments.title: OCR-induced typo in section reference
comments.quote: Sce. 4 details the experiments, and Sec. 5 gives the results.
comments.explanation: This appears to be an OCR artifact where 'Sce.' is a misread of 'Sec.'. The surrounding text consistently uses 'Sec.' for section references. This is not a conceptual or technical issue, as the intent is perfectly clear. Per instructions, OCR errors that are clearly resolvable (like this one) should not be flagged unless they would cause confusion even under the most plausible symbol correction. Here, 'Sce.' is clearly 'Sec.', so no real issue remains.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claimed k-mer count and frequency vector size
comments.quote: the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 − 3 + 1 = 1271. [...] length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261
comments.explanation: At first, this seems contradictory: the actual number of k-mers in a sequence is 1271 for k=3, but the frequency vector has length 9261. However, this is not an error — the frequency vector is a bag-of-k-mers representation over *all possible* 3-mers (21^3 = 9261), not the number of k-mers extracted. Each of the 1271 k-mers contributes to a count in the 9261-dimensional vector. This is standard in k-mer frequency methods. The text distinguishes these concepts: Section 3.2.1 correctly defines the frequency vector length as |Σ|^k. The initial confusion is resolved by context, so there is no real inconsistency.

comments.title: Misleading terminology in random error simulation
comments.quote: Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies
comments.explanation: The term 'point mutations' is biologically ambiguous here — in evolution, it refers to actual genetic changes, but here it refers to sequencing errors (artificial substitutions). The authors mean 'substitution errors', not mutations. Illumina sequencing errors are indeed primarily substitution-type, so the simulation is appropriate. While 'point mutation' is often used informally to mean a single nucleotide change regardless of origin, in a genomics context it usually implies a true biological change rather than an error. However, the context makes clear these are introduced errors for benchmarking, so the meaning is recoverable. The phrasing could mislead a less careful reader into thinking biological evolution is being modeled, but the adversarial example framing prevents serious confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Flipping' Amino Acids in Adversarial Examples
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the term 'flip their value randomly' is ambiguous. In molecular sequence contexts, 'flipping' typically implies substitution, but the exact mechanism — whether the new amino acid is chosen uniformly at random from all 21, excluding the original, or via a biologically plausible substitution matrix — is not specified. The authors intend to simulate sequencing errors, which are generally random substitutions, so uniform random substitution is a reasonable default. However, without specifying the distribution, this could lead to unrealistic perturbations (e.g., favoring certain amino acids due to sequencing bias). Context from Section 3.4 clarifies that these simulate Illumina errors, which are predominantly substitution errors, but still does not specify the substitution rule. This underspecification makes reproduction difficult, especially since different substitution schemes (e.g., uniform vs. codon-aware) can yield different robustness profiles. While experienced practitioners might assume uniform substitution, this should be stated explicitly for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Consecutive Errors' to Model Indels
comments.quote: In consecutive error generation, [...] we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio.
comments.explanation: The authors aim to simulate indel errors using 'consecutive amino acid flips', but this misrepresents indels. An insertion or deletion (indel) alters the sequence length and shifts downstream residues, whereas 'flipping' a consecutive subsequence replaces existing amino acids with others, preserving length — this is a block substitution, not an indel. While long-read technologies like PacBio do exhibit higher indel rates, the proposed perturbation does not mimic the structural consequences of indels on sequence alignment or downstream features like k-mer composition (which would see frame shifts or gain/loss of k-mers). The method may capture local error clustering, but calling it a simulation of indels is misleading. The authors could have instead inserted or deleted a small number of consecutive residues to better reflect PacBio's error profile. This is not just semantic: indels affect k-mer spectra more severely than substitutions, so the robustness test is weaker than claimed.

comments.title: Keras Classifier Architecture May Be Underpowered for Input Dimension
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
